title: Human Resources Consulting Services

evidence_facts.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 54161: Management Consulting Services
evidence_facts.quote: Providing advice and guidance concerning the development or modification of human resources strategies, policies, practices and procedures.

scope.population: Human resources management consulting services in the NAPCS product list
scope.industry_mismatch: Product-level classification for NAICS 541612; it does not provide a current firm count, revenue mix, price, labor mix, or adoption measure.

evidence_facts.title: The Role of AI in HR Continues to Expand
evidence_facts.quote: AI is transforming HR faster than ever: 43% of organizations now leverage AI in HR tasks, up from 26% in 2024.

scope.population: Organizations covered by SHRM 2025 Talent Trends research
scope.unit: percent of organizations leveraging AI in HR tasks
scope.industry_mismatch: End-user organization adoption in HR tasks, not adoption by U.S. NAICS 541612 consulting firms.

evidence_facts.title: Automation, Generative AI, and Job Displacement Risk in HR Employment
evidence_facts.quote: jobs in the HR field are substantially more likely to complete at least 50% of their tasks using GenAI tools, with 11.9% of HR employment estimated to reach or surpass that threshold

scope.population: U.S. HR employment aggregated across ten identified HR occupations
scope.unit: percent of HR employment estimated to have at least 50 percent of tasks completed using GenAI
scope.period: 2025 SHRM Automation/AI Survey using May 2024 OEWS employment data
scope.industry_mismatch: Occupation-level HR employment estimate, not a firm-level measure for NAICS 541612 or a consulting revenue measure.

evidence_facts.title: Instructions for Schedule SB (Form 5500)
evidence_facts.quote: An enrolled actuary must sign Schedule SB.

scope.population: Single-employer defined benefit plans subject to minimum funding standards
scope.industry_mismatch: Defined-benefit-plan filing rule; it applies only to a specialized benefits and actuarial subset rather than all NAICS 541612 services.

evidence_facts.title: OneDigital Welcomes Human Capital Concepts, Professional Employer Organization and HR Consulting Firm
evidence_facts.quote: OneDigital, the nation’s leading insurance brokerage, financial services, and HR consulting firm has acquired Human Capital Concepts (HCC), an Indianapolis-based IRS-Certified Professional Employer Organization (CPEO) and HR Consulting firm with over 6,600 worksite employees.

scope.population: OneDigital and acquired Human Capital Concepts
scope.geography: Indianapolis-based target; source says it serves the Midwest and greater United States
scope.unit: acquisition announcement and acquired worksite employees
scope.industry_mismatch: The target is a PEO and HR consulting firm with payroll, benefits, and other services; it is not demonstrated to be a pure NAICS 541612 consulting comparable.

evidence_facts.title: Alera Group Expands Services with Acquisition of Brio Benefits
evidence_facts.quote: Alera Group, a top independent national insurance and financial services firm, announced today the acquisition of Brio Benefits (Brio), an employee benefits consulting company based in New York.

scope.population: Alera Group and acquired Brio Benefits
scope.geography: United States; target based in New York
scope.industry_mismatch: Employee-benefits consulting target acquired by an insurance and financial-services firm; source does not isolate fee-only NAICS 541612 economics.

evidence_facts.title: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. Completes Acquisition of Buck
evidence_facts.quote: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. today announced it has completed the previously disclosed acquisition of the partnership interests of BCHR Holdings, L.P., dba Buck.

scope.population: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. and acquired Buck
scope.industry_mismatch: Acquirer is an insurance brokerage, risk-management, and consulting group; the source does not provide a pure NAICS 541612 deal multiple or a target revenue split.

evidence_facts.title: HUB International Strengthens Employee Benefits Services with Acquisition of Employee Benefit Associates, Inc. in Kentucky
evidence_facts.quote: Hub, a leading global insurance brokerage and financial services firm, announced today that it has acquired the assets of Employee Benefit Associates, Inc. (EBA).

scope.population: HUB International and acquired Employee Benefit Associates
scope.industry_mismatch: Employee Benefit Associates is described as an insurance agency with employee-benefit planning; it is not a pure NAICS 541612 consulting target.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541612 ($10,678,955,284) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541612 ($26,539,675,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $173,623 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541612); class <5: 5,881 firms, avg revenue/firm $394,584 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,752 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 651 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,244,538 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $194,770 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 412 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,382,619 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $372,880 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 431 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,933,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,241,542 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 144 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,228,493 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,574,259 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $121,304,852 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,984,209 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 575
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

scope_mismatches: F1 is a 2008 product definition and contains no task-time, employment, or wage-share data.
F2 measures end-user organizations using AI in HR tasks, not consulting-firm work or employee task shares.
F3 estimates HR employment rather than the target consulting workforce and uses occupation-level proximity methods.
F4 applies only to specialized defined-benefit-plan work, not the entire industry.
The deterministic role mix is 4-digit 5416 and omits distinctive compensation, benefits, and actuarial occupations.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to identify advice and guidance on HR policies and procedures as the target product context.
Use F2 and F3 only to establish AI use and material task exposure in the broader HR domain.
Use F4 only to retain human credentialed signing in a narrow defined-benefit subset.
Map documentation, analysis, policy, workflow, relationship, and credentialed-signoff tasks to each required supplied SOC without changing its wage share.
Apply a separate residual midpoint because the supplied roles do not identify the remaining wage share.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select role fractions that treat drafting, analysis, and workflow as highly assistable while retaining partner advice, client relationships, accountability, and specialized credentialed work.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Management-analysis documentation, data synthesis, benchmark summaries, and draft recommendations are exposed to AI assistance, while client diagnosis and executive judgment persist.
Internal planning and reporting can automate, but partner-level client relationships, accountability, and commercial judgment remain human-led.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Survey analysis, content, and market-research preparation are AI-exposed, while research design and client interpretation remain human work.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposal drafting, account research, and routine follow-up can automate, but relationship selling and negotiation remain human-led.
Engagement administration, status communication, and data collection can automate, while client-committee coordination and exception management remain.
Routine client service, routing, and information collection are AI-exposed, while escalations and relationship repair persist.
The broad supplied operations-specialist role is assumed to include analytics and process work that AI can assist, with controls and client judgment retained.
Software development and data-platform work can be materially AI-assisted, though architecture, security, and data-product responsibility remain.
Routine financial and compliance work can automate, while review, assurance, and client-facing explanation remain human work.
HR specialists perform policy, recruiting, and process work exposed to AI, but stakeholder judgment, sensitive conversations, and accountability remain.

The large unlisted wage remainder likely includes distinctive compensation, benefits, actuarial, analyst, and administrative roles absent from the supplied parent mix; use a broad midpoint rather than assign it a known composition.

pi_dist.rationale: Consulting advice, guidance, and some credentialed benefits work can support partial retention of AI productivity gains, but project and policy work is readily benchmarked by clients and the protected actuarial subset is narrow.

scope_mismatches: F1 defines advisory products but reports no pricing, payer, margin, contract term, or cost pass-through.
F4 is a filing-signature requirement for a specialized plan subset and does not state the share of industry revenue that is protected.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to classify the dominant service as client-facing advice and guidance rather than a standardized software sale.
Use F4 to recognize a limited credentialed workstream where an AI-only delivery cannot satisfy the filing requirement.
Do not use either source as direct evidence of fee retention or a price change.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Choose 0.45 as partial retention: data, recurring-cycle, and credentialed work may retain savings, while open-ended advisory analysis is vulnerable to client repricing and self-service.

pi_moat.rationale: The code spans generalized policy and procedure advice alongside a smaller credentialed defined-benefit subset. The latter is defensible; the former has lower barriers and is exposed to AI-enabled HR platforms and self-service.

scope_mismatches: F1 does not measure firm differentiation, proprietary data, client switching, or revenue concentration.
F4 applies to a particular defined-benefit-plan requirement and cannot quantify the portion of NAICS 541612 revenue behind it.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to distinguish policy and procedure advice from other management consulting products.
Use F4 to identify a human signature gate in a specialized benefits subsegment.
Treat data-product, privilege, recurring-contract, and relationship effects as unmeasured rather than established facts.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Choose a mid-low moat estimate because credentialed benefits work and relationships persist but the code contains substantial generalized advisory work that can be competed away or internally produced.

t50_years.rationale: AI use in the broader HR domain is already material, but the target is consulting firms rather than end-user HR departments and a credentialed subsegment remains non-substitutable for signing.

scope_mismatches: F2 measures organizations using AI in HR tasks, not U.S. NAICS 541612 firms materially using AI in automatable functions.
F3 is an occupation-level HR employment estimate, not a consulting-firm adoption measurement or diffusion forecast.
F4 applies only to a defined-benefit-plan subset and does not forecast AI adoption.
bridge_steps: Use F2 and F3 to establish that AI use and task-level exposure in HR are already meaningful but incomplete.
Use F4 to identify one source of persistent human signoff in a limited subsegment.
Keep current consulting-firm adoption below 50 percent because no target-population measure was located.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Forecast 3.5 years from the run date to 50 percent material adoption by target firms; this is a diffusion judgment, not an observed forecast.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: SHRM reports meaningful AI use in end-user HR and material task use in HR employment, but neither fact observes the share of NAICS 541612 firms materially using AI in their own automatable functions.

scope_mismatches: F2 reports end-user organizations using AI in HR tasks and does not identify consulting firms or material use threshold.
F3 estimates workers and task intensity across HR occupations, rather than consulting-firm AI adoption.
bridge_steps: Use F2 as an upper-context indicator of broader HR-domain AI penetration.
Use F3 to confirm that broad HR task use is not synonymous with majority-of-task automation.
Define the target strictly as U.S. NAICS 541612 firms materially using AI in automatable functions.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 25 percent as a lower-than-end-user adoption estimate for target consulting firms, without treating the 43 percent end-user figure as directly transferable.

historical_analogs.value: The relevant analogue is digitization of HR analysis and policy workflow: routine drafting, research, and process administration can be augmented or standardized, while high-stakes client counsel and credentialed pension signoff remain separate human services.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources establish the product scope, current HR AI usage, task exposure, and a narrow credentialed constraint; they do not provide a historical consulting-price or margin study.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Workflow augmentation to substitution of standardized advisory deliverables

scope_mismatches: F1 is a product definition, not a historical technology outcome study.
F2 and F3 are current HR-domain adoption and task evidence, not consulting-firm financial analogs.
F4 is a legal filing requirement for a narrow subset, not a general service-economics observation.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to identify advisory policy and procedure deliverables.
Use F2 and F3 to identify AI-enabled workflow and task exposure in the HR domain.
Use F4 to separate credentialed signoff from generically automatable work.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Describe partial workflow substitution as an analogue without asserting an observed historical price, labor, or valuation effect.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No directly applicable, live, owner-age distribution for U.S. NAICS 541612 consulting firms was located in the assigned research scope.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because no target-population owner-age statistic was located
bridge_steps: Searched public HR consulting, professional-services, and association materials for owner age or 60-plus distributions.
Located no live source in this run that measures owners of U.S. NAICS 541612 consulting firms at the requested threshold.
Avoid mapping employee, HR-professional, or broad small-business demographics to the target owner population.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Use 25 percent only as a broad planning estimate for boutique consulting owners, not as an observed demographic statistic.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No directly verified source located in this run documents a succession shortage for owners of U.S. NAICS 541612 consulting firms.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because this is an absence-of-located-documentation judgment
bridge_steps: Searched public HR consulting, professional-services, and association materials for succession, retirement, owner-age, and shortage evidence.
Located no direct statement documenting a NAICS 541612 owner succession shortage.
Do not infer a shortage from merger activity, executive-search demand, or generic professional-services retirement claims.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Set false as an absence-of-evidence estimate, not as proof that no shortage exists.

active_consolidators.rationale: Four separately evidenced large platforms completed acquisitions of HR, employee-benefits, or people-advisory businesses. The count is deliberately limited to visible, named platforms and is not a NAICS 541612 census.

scope_mismatches: F5 target is a PEO and HR consulting firm with broader payroll and benefits services.
F6 acquirer is an insurance and financial-services firm and target economics may include brokerage-related revenue.
F7 acquirer is an insurance brokerage, risk-management, and consulting group; no pure target revenue split is reported.
F8 target is described as an insurance agency with employee-benefit planning rather than a pure HR consultancy.
bridge_steps: Treat OneDigital, Alera Group, Arthur J. Gallagher, and HUB International as four separate named platforms with evidenced people-and-benefits adjacent acquisitions.
Use the count only as a conservative proxy for visible consolidation pressure.
Exclude unverified platforms, local buyers, and any claim that the four represent all NAICS 541612 acquirers.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select four visible consolidator-like platforms while explicitly excluding brokerage-led deal economics from any direct valuation inference.

buy_mult.rationale: No live comparable set located in this run states an EV/EBITDA purchase multiple after market-rate owner compensation for a fee-based, acquirable NAICS 541612 target.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because the located acquisition announcements do not disclose target EV/EBITDA, owner-compensation normalization, or fee-only consulting mix
bridge_steps: Searched public people-and-benefits acquisition materials for completed target EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation.
Located no usable comparable for a transferable $1-10M EBITDA fee-based HR consulting target.
Do not use insurance-brokerage transaction values, PEO values, revenue figures, or platform enterprise values as a direct consulting multiple.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Use 4.0x only as a low-confidence underwriting placeholder for a diversified fee-based consulting target after hypothetical owner-compensation normalization.

exit_mult.rationale: No live comparable set located in this run supports an AI-era exit EV/EBITDA multiple after market-rate owner compensation, so the small step-up is only a conditional scenario.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used because no normalized exit comparables, forward transaction terms, or target fee-mix disclosures were located
bridge_steps: Retain the buy-multiple target definition: a transferable fee-based HR consulting firm with market-rate owner compensation.
Treat AI as a potential workflow margin benefit only if advisory demand and proprietary-data or credentialed services remain durable.
Do not import brokerage, PEO, or broad-platform valuation data into the fee-based consulting multiple.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Use 4.3x as a small scenario premium to the 4.0x buy assumption, contingent on a durable recurring or differentiated service mix.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed project, retainer, recurring advisory-cycle, and data or survey subscription arrangements; the revenue shares and sensitivity of each fee type to AI are unverified in this run.
pricing_structure.rationale: The product definition supports multiple advice-and-guidance deliverables and the filing rule supports a specialized recurring compliance service, but neither source reports contract terms, payers, prices, or mix.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Predominantly project and retainer fees, with a smaller to material recurring data or compliance component

scope_mismatches: F1 provides product definitions rather than fee basis, contract duration, payer, price, or revenue shares.
F4 provides a filing requirement rather than a service-pricing observation or industry mix.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to identify policy, procedure, compensation, and benefits advisory products.
Use F4 to recognize a potential annual compliance-signoff workstream in a limited benefits subset.
Do not claim that any listed pricing stream is observed or dominant.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select a mixed-fee description as a low-confidence industry-structure assumption; revenue mix and pass-through are left explicitly unmeasured.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Python supplies uplift_pp from the role-weighted labor exposure. The 3.5-year adoption estimate reflects meaningful HR-domain AI use and task exposure, while retaining a lag for target consulting-firm diffusion and specialized human signoff.

terminal_value.justification: AI can reduce the cost of analysis, drafting, policy, and workflow deliverables, and client HR functions already use it. Paid demand remains for relationship-based advice and defined-benefit work requiring an enrolled actuary, but the protected share of this heterogeneous code is not measured and generalized advisory output is vulnerable to self-service.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V and A use only HR product, AI-use, task-exposure, and credential-context evidence plus explicitly labeled adoption and timing judgments. Deal evidence informs active-consolidator context only and is excluded from V and A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The mixed fee model maps AI savings only partially into pi_dist because recurring or credentialed work may retain some value, while open project advisory and unmeasured data-product competition lower pi_moat. No fee deflation or margin gain is asserted as observed.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5416 and is management-consulting dominated. It omits distinctive compensation, benefits, and actuarial occupations and includes roles that may be weighted differently at the six-digit code. The deterministic labor share may also mix pass-through, brokerage-adjacent, or subcontracted economics. Required SOCs and wage shares are used unchanged, so the role assessment and margin interpretation are low confidence.

sensitivity.alternative_value: Use a validated six-digit HR consulting mix with compensation, benefits, and actuarial roles
sensitivity.impact: V and final uplift_pp could move materially because the parent-code roles and residual do not identify the target workforce.

sensitivity.alternative_value: Higher service labor intensity after separating pass-through, brokerage-adjacent, or subcontracted receipts
sensitivity.impact: V and final uplift_pp would change with the deterministic labor-share limitation; no adjustment is made here.

subsegments.name: HR policy, organizational-development, and talent advisory
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Selected as dominant because the NAPCS definition covers HR strategies, policies, practices, and procedures; no public six-digit revenue split was located in this run.

subsegments.name: Compensation, benefits, retirement, and actuarial consulting
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: The NAPCS product list explicitly covers compensation and benefits consulting, and Schedule SB demonstrates a credentialed defined-benefit subset; revenue share remains unmeasured.

subsegments.name: HR consulting and implementation-service bundles
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: The product list distinguishes advice-only services from bundled implementation; no firm-level split or separate profitability evidence was located.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: HR policy, organizational-development, and talent advisory
market_boundary.selection_basis: Score generalized HR advisory because it is the broadest advice-and-guidance definition in the located product classification. The choice is a low-confidence market-boundary judgment because published six-digit revenue shares were not located.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A changes directly with the adoption-timing estimate.
The source measures end-user HR organizations rather than consulting firms, so the target adoption base could materially differ.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Validated six-digit mix unavailable; within-role ranges are 0.10-0.75
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: V and final uplift_pp are highly sensitive to the parent-code mix, omitted actuarial and compensation roles, and residual allocation.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Open project work through recurring data, credentialed, or compliance work
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: C depends on the unknown revenue mix that determines whether AI savings are retained or services are repriced or self-served.
M depends on unobserved normalized target multiples; brokerage and PEO deal values are not comparable.

reviewer_note: All selected values are estimates because the live sources establish product scope, broader HR AI use, task exposure, a narrow filing-signature rule, or people-and-benefits acquisition activity rather than target NAICS firm-level measures. No target owner-age statistic, fee-mix dataset, or market-rate owner-compensated transaction comparable was located in this run.

V.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: V_raw = labor_share × ai_replaceable_share = 0.429 × 0.4989 = 0.2140281; V = 10 × min(1, V_raw / 0.25) = 8.561124

C.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat = 10 × 0.45 × 0.45 = 2.025

A.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: A = 10 when t50_years = 0, otherwise min(10, 10 / t50_years); t50_years = 3.5, A = 2.85714285714

M.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: M = clamp(4 × (exit_mult − buy_mult) / buy_mult, 0, 10) = 0.3
